speaker: interviewer
text: --Cohn in Dallas, Texas, United States of America. Mr. Cohn, when we had stopped, you were discussing finding a new position and you were getting ready to board a bus.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Hold on. Hold on. I need to stop.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And so we would have to take a bus, an Arab bus. And they said, for your risk you are taking, you know, I'll pay you, instead of nine piastres, I'll pay you 15, I think, piastres. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So we took a bus every morning to the City of Acre, an Arab city, and we painted in a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] government building, on the inside. And also, once we painted in a jail. There's a big, notorious jail at that time in Acre, in that town. And so I painted one of the cells. It's a very ancient-- very ancient. It was a fortress, you know. A very ancient fortress going back to very-- to Phoenician times. You know, to Biblical, to pre-Biblical times. And so I once painted a cell. And they have these arched ceilings. And while I painted it, I dislocated my shoulder. So that was the end of that job. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had mentioned that it was dangerous to take this bus. Why was it dangerous?

speaker: interviewee
text: The danger? To get killed by the Arabs. You know, stabbed in the back or shot or beaten to death.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did--

speaker: interviewee
text: But I-- somehow I always didn't feel that way. Once I went, when we worked in that government building, and during a lunch break I went out for a walk in the city. And people waved to me and smiled to me, you know. And I felt they were all friendly. You know, I didn't feel any reason to be afraid, but, I mean, there was.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so when I came back to work after lunch, the painter, the master-- he was a master painter from Poland. He was a real painter. And he said, where have you been? So I say, I took a walk. Are you crazy? Don't ever do that again. You know? You want to get killed? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And so, anyway, so that was the end of that job. And then I-- until I got [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] recuperated from the dislocation. Then I worked all sorts of jobs where I could get, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were relationships like between Jews and non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it was a kind of a war, a war situation. A civil war situation. For instance, I once had a job for a day in a cemetery, a Jewish cemetery, outside Haifa. And I [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] dug around the trees, you know. And the next morning there was a funeral of some Jewish high official or so, you know, from the union, I think. And so in one of the-- probably in one of the woods that I made there, there was a bomb. There was a bomb in there, and this to be exploded from about half a mile away or so from under a tree. And, fortunately, they found it. It was found before it was exploded. So it was a very hostile situation. On the other hand, in some parts of the cities, you know, Jews and Arabs were living together peacefully. You know, it was very-- it wasn't ambiguous. Not always. The road were very-- the roads were very insecure, you know. Buses were often attacked. And, as I said before, we had to drive to work to the [INAUDIBLE]. You know, we drove to work with iron-- with, yeah, metal plates on the sides of the trucks. And, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the British played a very-- a very ambiguous political game, you know? They tried to play one against the other, like they usually do, you know? Like cause-- divide and rule, you know. That was their politics all the time. What were your relationships like with non-Jews?

speaker: interviewer
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your relationships like with non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: With non-Jews? In Palestine?

speaker: interviewer
text: In Palestine.

speaker: interviewee
text: None. There were really no relationships when we-- there were-- there was the British establishment, which we had no connection with whatsoever. Maybe some higher classes, you know, had some social connections with the higher-- with the higher officials of the British government. But, otherwise, the average citizen had no-- except when you was arrested, you know, for something. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was the Haganah, the self-defense organization, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which trained-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which was trained for defense, you know. And they were illegal. So if they were caught and they were arrested, you know, sent to jail or so. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And otherwise, there were the Arabs. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Most of them were Christian Arabs. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And people like me, for instance, we had no personal connection. A friend of mine did. He worked-- we became friends. We met in the training farm in Italy. And they immigrated about the same time I did, I think. And so we made friends, but they went to a different kind of kibbutz. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then they left that kibbutz also, and they also went to Haifa. And we remained friends. Very close friends. And they were also very poor, you know. They were a couple. And I once stole for them a chair. [LAUGHS] They had no furniture, so I stole this chair-- stole the chair for them. And he worked. He was-- he had been a druggist or something. And he had made experiments and had [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] destroyed some of his glands. So his skin were constantly red and peeling. You know, he had no fat-- the fat glands, they were destroyed. And he wasn't very-- wasn't a very strong man. And his wife, she was, also, she was an intelligent woman. She knew several languages. And he knew some Arabic. He had already studied in Germany. And he worked for the government, public works. And they employed mostly Arabs, but he got a job there, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: And several times Arabs saved his life. They went-- when they were attacked by terrorists, they threw-- they threw-- you know this Arab headdress? Keffiyeh? They threw a keffiyeh over him so that he appeared like an Arab. So they saved-- in three instances he saved-- they saved his life. Arabs. Were you able to learn any Arabic phrases?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you speak fluent?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you speak fluent Arabic?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fluent? No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wanted to go back for a second. Where did you find this chair for them?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] In a-- I belonged to a sport club in Haifa. And it was a big club, you know, and there were lots of chairs, you know. So I took one. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do at the sports club?

speaker: interviewee
text: I belonged to a sea sport group. You know, sailing. A sailing group. And actually mainly because my friend who was a trainer for-- he was a wrestler. And born in Germany, yeah, Berlin. And he trained. He was a trainer in that club. And he advised me, he said, join the club because they could also find you jobs. Yeah. That's why I actually joined. And I joined the sea-- the sea sport club. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go sailing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go sailing? Did you go sailing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sailing? Yeah. Yeah. And also we-- at the end shortly before the war broke out-- there was-- they conducted a course in Tel Aviv for-- officially a sailing course. But in reality, it was a course to train people to smuggle in immigrants, to run the British blockade, because they didn't allow any Jewish immigration anymore. And so they had an organization that stretched all over Europe and they brought in refugees from the Nazi-occupied countries to Palestine. And they had to run the British blockade. And that course was to train people to work in that network. And so I went to a course also. I had no work, so I went to the course. And at the end of the course, we had a practice run. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We emptied out a ship from fish. There was hundreds of-- I don't know how many hundreds of refugees were on the ship. I have a-- I made a drawing a couple of years ago from my memory [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how we-- how that [INAUDIBLE]. We took a ship in at night. You know, we a little-- we had this lamp, a kerosene lamp, in the rocks. We had-- there was a rocky coast. In the rocks we had a lamp so the ship followed that light and came in as much-- as close to the coast as it could have. And then it dropped anchor and then we swim out-- no. No. They sent one of the lifeboats. And we boarded that lifeboat and rowed back to the ship. And then we took them out of the lifeboats and filled them up with refugees. And I rowed one of the boats. It was so full, we couldn't-- there was no room in the boat. We put in as many people as we could. So I sat at the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] front end of the boat and rowed. And I had one foot on a refugee, on the back of a refugee, and one foot on a piece of luggage. And so we rowed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, suddenly, a light came up in the night-- at night, you know? Suddenly a light projector lighted up the whole scene. It was a police boat from the British police. And so we all-- we all tried to get away, you know? [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] So, anyway, we scrambled back to the shore. You know, the ship was already empty. They had already emptied all of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then there was one guy-- one of our guys was left on the ship. And he called, I can't swim. I can't swim. So they told him, get a life jacket or life, you know, belt, and jump in. And supposedly he did. And supposedly he was-- then, when we came to shore, and the light-- the boat left, the police boat somehow left us. And then we swim out. We all swim out in a big line to search for him. Well, we didn't find him. So we were told next morning that he was-- that he drifted onto the shore someplace. But we were never sure whether he did or not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you stopped by the police that night?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. They just somehow disappeared. Maybe they were bribed. Could be they were bribed, you know? Because they were very given to bribery. That was the British police.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever able to assist any other refugees?

speaker: interviewee
text: To what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to assist any other refugees in smuggling them in?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. In smuggling them in, that was the only time. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did the refugees go from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because then-- because after that course I got very sick. At the end of the course I got very sick. I got, what's it called? Paratyphoid. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then the course was at an end. And-- I think we can [INAUDIBLE]. It takes too much time. No?

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were sick at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I was sick, and I was in the hospital for-- [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] for diseases that-- what do you call it? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Infectious diseases. And when I came out, the course was over. Nobody was there anymore, and I had left all my belongings there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I had nothing. I had no clothes, no money. Somebody lent-- they found some old clothes from somebody who had died there probably. I had no socks. I had a pair of shoes. I had old-- they gave me a pair of old shoes. And they game me money to take the bus to Tel Aviv. And then I wrote to the offices of that-- of the Labor Federation, you know, that was organized by them. And, anyway, I got stuck in Tel Aviv without money after being-- having been sick. [LAUGHS] And, but my uncle lived in Tel Aviv, so I went-- walked all the way. I walked to them, and then they gave me some money so that I could go back to Haifa by bus-- by-- no. Buses were not running anymore. It was Friday evening, you know, and buses don't run there on Friday evening or on Saturdays. And so I had to take a cab. You know, there were cabs that drive between cities. You know, took six people or seven people, you know, and like a, you know, interurban service kind of-- of taxis.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had nothing with me. I had no paper or anything. And so they told me, you got your identity card? Because everybody had to have an identity card, and there were check posts. And I said, no, I don't have anything. So you can't go, he says. They'll take you in prison. They put you in prison. So, but I have to go. I have nothing here. All the other rest of my things are in Haifa. I have no money, nothing here. Said, okay. You take a chance. So I said, yeah, I take a chance. So before Haifa, there's this checkpoint. There was a check post, and they check everybody, you know, for identity cards because of, you know, of terrorism, you know. And so I was sitting in the back of the cab and the policeman, an Arab policeman, stuck his head in and, you know, they asked for the identity cards. And somehow he overlooked me and I got through. [LAUGHS] Yeah. What happened when you-- what happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, next I-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I bought myself a bottle of wine and eggs, and I mixed it and I drank it. And this way I got my strength back. And then the war broke out. September 1st, 1939. And I had no money left, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so I wanted to join the foreign legion, the French foreign legion.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to the French consulate and he said, OK. You have a passport? I said, no. My German passport wasn't valid, you know. So I put that-- I put there some kind of a status passport, I think. If you bring your passport, travel-- travel-- we take you to Syria. You know, I would have to go to Syria to the foreign legion there.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I didn't have money. That cost, I think, half a pound. And I didn't have money. So I continued walking, and then I came to a place in the former German colony. And there was a row of people standing-- of men standing there, waiting.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said, what are you waiting for? They said, ah, for the army, work, for the army, or something. They weren't clear. So I said, oh, that's probably to pacify the people, because there was so many unemployment. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then I met a friend, another friend, and he said, why don't you register? Register, what can-- you know, so I stood in line and finally I came to the officer that registered, and it was a registration for the army. For the British army. So I registered.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after a few days, I got-- the policeman came up with a notification that I should come to the physical, you know. And so there were a lot of people there, and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] about 60 or 70 people there. And from all the 60 or 70 people, they accepted four. Three Arab and me. So that's how I got in the army.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the first thing I got was 25 piastre. That was a quarter of a pound. You know, like, well, let's see. How much would that be here? At this time, maybe five dollar. And that was-- at least I had some money. [LAUGHS] Where did you serve in the British Army?

speaker: interviewee
text: In North Africa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you went to North Africa, were you able to maintain communication with your family while you were living in Palestine, back in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, through my uncle who lived in Tel Aviv. He was a doctor. A doctor there. And-- my father's brother. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I at that time, after the war broke out-- before the war I was still in constant correspondence with my parents. Yes? And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we wrote frequently, you know, so I always knew what was going on with them. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: But then when the war broke out, there was no communication possible between enemy countries. And the only way was to communicate through the international Red Cross. And they had for-- cards, Red Cross cards, a whole form, where you put your name, and the name of the address where you wanted to send it, and then, I think, 25 words. We were allowed to write 25 words. And this would be then transmitted to the family-- to whoever, you know, you wrote to.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- my mother-- my father was dead already by-- no, my-- no. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] My father died in '40, 1940. And the war broke out. Yeah, but he died in January, 1940. And the war broke out in September, 1939. So then after my father died, my mother wrote these postcards. And I also have one where I wrote and asked her, we haven't heard from you and I miss hearing from you. And so she answered back to that card, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the card, that last card, was from the Warsaw Ghetto. And that I got when I was in-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in the desert, North Africa. And we were standing by for the Battle of El Alamein that fought the Germans from North Africa, the Afrika Korps, the Rommel. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] And that's where my uncle sent-- he didn't know where I was, either, but he sent through the army, you know, with my name and rank and company. So I got that card when we were standing there waiting for that, for that battle. That was the last I heard from my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll need to take a tape break.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.
